coredata.dc:title: CQDs-MoS2 QDs loaded on Dendritic fibrous Nanosilica/Hydrophobic waterborne polyurethane acrylate for antibacterial coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Jianzhong
Liu, Chenyan
Yan, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Since bacterial infections are the cause of many diseases, death, and disability, considerable attention has been paid to the preparation of antibacterial surfaces. Anti-adhesive and bactericidal synergistic surfaces show promising antimicrobial applications due to the removal and killing effect of microorganisms. Herein, we proposed a leather coating surface with hydrophobic and photocatalytic antibacterial properties. Dendritic fibrous nanosilica (DFNS) loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and MoS2 QDs (DFNS@CQDs-MoS2) nanoparticles were successfully prepared, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic antibacterial activity. Specifically, the DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles exhibited antibacterial activities of 99.98%, 95.35%, and 99.99% against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Aspergillus niger, respectively. Then, hydrophobic waterborne polyurethane acrylate (HWPUA) was developed, which exhibited enhanced bacterial adhesion resistance. Furthermore, the HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coating completely inactivated the residual bacterial cells. Therefore, the synergistic antibacterial coatings developed in this work could have potential applications as antibacterial surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dendritic fibrous nanosilica
CQDs
MoS2 QDs
Antibacterial surfaces

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of DFNS and DFNS embedded CQDs and MoS2 QDs (DFNS@CQDs-MoS2)
Synthesis of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 emulsions
Preparation of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes
Antibacterial properties of nanoparticles and coated leather
Morphology of bacterial adhesion on the treated leather
Morphology and structure of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes
Antibacterial activity of nanoparticles and coated leather

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFB0308602 ), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51803112 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) CQDs and (b) MoS2 QDs. TEM images of (c) DFNS and (d, e) DFNS@ CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles. (f) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (g) BJH pore size distribution curves of DFNS and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2. (h) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, DFNS and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2. (i) Elemental mapping images of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles.

ESR spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) MoS2 QDs, and (c) DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 under ambient light (red curves) and xenon light irradiation for 15 min (black curves). (d) Schematic image of photocatalytic mechanism of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2.
(a) 1H NMR spectrum of HWPUA. (b) Strain-stress curves (insets are photographs of water contact angles) and (c) FT-IR spectra of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes.
Representative colony formation of (A) S. aureus, (B) E. coli, and (C) A. niger on LB agar plates in the presence of (a) normal saline, (b) MoS2 QDs, (c) CQDs, and (d) DFNS@CQDs-MoS2.

(A) SEM and (B) TEM images of (a) S. aureus cells, (b) E. coli cells, and (c) A. niger cells irradiated with light (0.3 W/cm2 xenon lamp) for 15 min, and (a1) S. aureus cells, (b1) E. coli cells, and (c1) A. niger cells treated with DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 and irradiated with light for 15 min.
Photographs of inhibition zone of (A) S. aureus, (B) E. coli, and (C) A. niger treated with (a) uncoated leather, (b) HWPUA coated leather, and (c) HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coated leather.
SEM images of (a) uncoated leather, (b) HWPUA coated leather, and (c) HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coated leather treated with (A) S. aureus, (B) E. coli, and (C) A. niger.

head.ce:title: CQDs-MoS2 QDs loaded on Dendritic fibrous Nanosilica/Hydrophobic waterborne polyurethane acrylate for antibacterial coatings

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since bacterial infections are the cause of many diseases, death, and disability, considerable attention has been paid to the preparation of antibacterial surfaces. Anti-adhesive and bactericidal synergistic surfaces show promising antimicrobial applications due to the removal and killing effect of microorganisms. Herein, we proposed a leather coating surface with hydrophobic and photocatalytic antibacterial properties. Dendritic fibrous nanosilica (DFNS) loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and MoS2 QDs (DFNS@CQDs-MoS2) nanoparticles were successfully prepared, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic antibacterial activity. Specifically, the DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles exhibited antibacterial activities of 99.98%, 95.35%, and 99.99% against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Aspergillus niger, respectively. Then, hydrophobic waterborne polyurethane acrylate (HWPUA) was developed, which exhibited enhanced bacterial adhesion resistance. Furthermore, the HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coating completely inactivated the residual bacterial cells. Therefore, the synergistic antibacterial coatings developed in this work could have potential applications as antibacterial surfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious diseases have been the leading cause of disease, death, and disability for centuries, and are a significant challenge to human progress and survival [1]. Over millions of years, microorganisms have developed adaptive mechanisms of biofilm formation by colonization on the surface of substances [2]. Bacteria embedded in the biofilms can survive in hostile environments and they are extremely difficult to remove due to the weak antibacterial sensitivity and ability to produce resistant strains [3,4]. The bacterial biofilms colonized on the surfaces are known to cause infection and affect the function of various surfaces, such as leather, textiles, plastic, and medical implants [5]. Therefore, it is of great significance to design and fabricate antibacterial surfaces that can resist bacterial adhesion and prevent biofilm formation.

Antibacterial surfaces can be categorized as anti-adhesive surfaces and bactericidal surfaces [6]. Anti-adhesive surfaces can repel the initial attachment of bacteria and prevent bacterial growth. Various approaches to prepare anti-adhesive surfaces have been extensively investigated over the past decades, which include the development of hydrophobic or superhydrophobic surfaces [7,8]. These surfaces cause the water drop to rotate due to the presence of an air layer between the liquid–solid interface, taking away the bacteria and making it difficult for bacteria to adhere to the surface. Although these anti-adhesive surfaces may resist or prevent cellular attachment, the reduction of bacterial infections is insufficient. Bactericidal surfaces can inactivate the cells attached to the surfaces, causing cell death. The research on antibacterial agents is promising for the development of bactericidal surfaces, such as organic bactericidal substances (chitosan, quaternary ammonium salts, and N-chloramines) [9,10], metals (Ag, Cu, and Au) [11,12], and metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, and Cu2O) [13]. However, multidrug-resistant bacteria are evolving at an alarming rate with the emergence of sterilizing materials. Neither independent anti-adhesive surface nor bactericidal surface can completely resist the growth of bacterial biofilms. Composite surfaces with both hydrophobic bacterial repelling property and bactericidal activity show a reduced probability of bacteria developing biofilms and multidrug-resistance due to multiple antibacterial mechanisms [14,15].

Since carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared, they have received extensive attention in sensing of metal ions and molecules, bioimaging, and photocatalysis, due to their easy synthesis and modification, excellent fluorescence performance, high biocompatibility, and good water dispersibility [16,17]. However, the studies on their antibacterial properties have only recently emerged [18,19]. The antibacterial mechanism of CQDs involves the positive charge of surface and the reactive oxygen species (ROS), which induces oxidative stress and causes damage to cell membrane, protein, DNA, and/or RNA. [20–22]. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance is limited due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. As a typical layered transition metal disulfide, MoS2 QDs show good photocatalytic performance and effective antibacterial properties [23–25]. The recombination of the CQDs and MoS2 improves the photocatalytic antibacterial performance due to the photogenerated charges existing in different semiconductor energy bands and the effective separation of the photocarriers [26,27]. However, quantum dots easily aggregate and even lose their photoactivity and antibacterial activity [28]. Compared with traditional mesoporous silica, dendritic fibrous nanosilica (DFNS) exhibits a special center-radiating mesoporous structure, high pore volume, and large specific surface area. The V-shaped pores provide the DFNS nanoparticles with more contact sites for convenient loading and diffusion of the guest molecules [29,30]. Therefore, DFNS loaded with quantum dots exhibits increased dispersion and larger contact area between CQDs and MoS2 QDs, which can promote the transfer of photogenerated electrons [31–33].

Herein, synergistic bactericidal and anti-adhesive surfaces were investigated based on the developed DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles doped into hydrophobic waterborne polyurethane acrylate (HWPUA), as shown in Fig. 1 . Using DFNS as carrier platform loaded with CQDs and MoS2 QDs can not only solve the problem of aggregation but can also improve the photocatalytic activity and ROS generation by rapid transfer of the photogenerated electrons. The morphologies of Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and Aspergillus niger (A. niger) bacterial cells after treatment with the developed DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles were investigated. The hydrophobic surface repelled most bacteria and hindered the formation of bacterial biofilms. More importantly, HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coated leather exhibited excellent bactericidal activity and anti-adhesive properties.

Cetylpyridinium bromide, 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine, sodium molybdate dehydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), L-glutathione, 2,2-dimethylol-butanoic acid, hydroxyethyl acrylate, potassium persulfate, and stearyl methacrylate were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Chitosan was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Spermidine and dibutyltin dilaurate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetraethyl orthosilicate, urea, triethylamine, acetic acid, cyclohexane, and n-butanol were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Isophorone diisocyanate was supplied by Bayer. Poly (caprolactone glycol) 1000 was supplied by Taisen Chemical Group. 2,2,3,4,4,4-Hexafluorobutyl methacrylate was provided by Harbin Xuejia Fluorosilicone Chemical Co., Ltd.

CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method [34]. In a typical procedure, 0.6 g of chitosan and 1.1 g of acetic acid were dissolved in deionized water and ultrasonicated until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and treated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the yellow supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min. Then, 10 mL of supernatant and 0.03 g spermidine were mixed and transferred to a reactor and treated at 260 °C for 2 h. Finally, the brownish-yellow supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min.

MoS2 QDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [35]. Typically, 0.50 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O was mixed with 40 mL deionized water and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.5 with 0.1 M HCl. Afterward, a premixed emulsion of glutathione (1.0 g L-glutathione and 100 mL deionized water) was injected into the solution and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and treated at 200 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min, the yellow MoS2 QDs solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DFNS and DFNS embedded CQDs and MoS2 QDs (DFNS@CQDs-MoS2)
ce:section.ce:para: DFNS was synthesized by a method previously reported in the literature [36,37]. In brief, a mixture containing 1.5 g cetylpyridinium bromide, 0.6 g urea, and 45 mL deionized water was ultrasonicated until a transparent solution was obtained. Then, a premixed solution containing 45 mL cyclohexane, 3.75 g tetraethyl orthosilicate, and 1.38 mL n-butanol was added to the above solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Thereafter, the mixture was treated at 70 °C for 10 h, centrifuged, and washed with acetone and water. The white DFNS powder was obtained by calcination of the product at 550 °C for 5 h. DFNS (0.3 g) was dispersed in 10 mL of CQDs suspension and 10 mL of MoS2 QDs suspension, and vigorously stirred for 6 h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed with water, and dried at room temperature to obtain the yellow DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: HWPUA was prepared by emulsion copolymerization. First, poly(caprolactone glycol) 1000 was dried at 120 °C. Then, 1.94 g dried poly(caprolactone glycol) 1000 and 0.53 g 2,2-dimethylol-butanoic acid were injected into a four-necked flask and stirred at 300 rpm at 60 °C for 0.5 h. Then, 2.22 g isophorone diisocyanate and dibutyltin dilaurate catalyst were injected into the mixture, which was then treated at 80 °C for 3 h. Afterward, 0.89 g of hydroxyethyl acrylate was added as a capping agent and the mixture was stirred at 40 °C for another 1 h. Thereafter, stearyl methacrylate, 2,2,3,4,4,4-hexafluorobutyl methacrylate or/and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles were added into the obtained polymer solution to reduce the viscosity for 1 h. To neutralize the carboxyl group in 2,2-dimethylol-butanoic acid, 0.36 g triethylamine was injected. After 0.5 h, 24 mL of deionized water was dropped into the mixture and stirred at 1000 rpm to obtain an emulsion. Then, 0.03 g of potassium persulfate was dropped into the emulsion, which was additionally treated at 75 °C for 3 h. The milky HWPUA or HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 emulsion was obtained by continuous stirring at 80 °C for another 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes
ce:section.ce:para: HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 emulsions were poured into polytetrafluoroethylene templates. The membranes were obtained after drying at 30 °C for 48 h and then kept in a desiccator for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties of nanoparticles and coated leather
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial activity of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles was evaluated by the plate count method. Gram-negative E. coli, Gram-positive S. aureus, and fungus A. niger were selected as representative bacterial strains. Colonies of E. coli and S. aureus strain were picked from the Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plate and inoculated in LB medium (20 mL). A. niger was inoculated in 20 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. Bacteria suspensions were grown under shaking (370 rpm) at 37 °C for 18 h. The suspensions were diluted by PBS solution to obtain 106 CFU/mL bacteria samples. To evaluate the antibacterial properties of the prepared materials, CQDs (150 μg/mL), MoS2 QDs (150 μg/mL), and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles (10 mg/mL) were cultivated with bacteria. Then, the samples were irradiated with a xenon lamp (0.3 W/cm2) for 15 min and shaken for 2 h. Normal saline was used as blank control. After incubation at 37 °C for 12 h, images of samples were taken and colonies were calculated. Each experiment was repeated three times.

Determination of zone of inhibition (ZOI) was used to measure the antibacterial properties of uncoated and coated leather. E. coli, S. aureus, and A.niger bacteria suspensions of 106 CFU/mL were dropped onto LB agar plate. Then, disk-shaped leather samples (uncoated leather, HWPUA coated leather, and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coated leather) with 20 mm diameter were placed on the surface of LB agar plate. Then, the plates were inoculated at 37 °C for 24 h.

E. coli, S. aureus, and A. niger cells were treated with normal saline and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles (10 mg/mL) as in the experiment above, and collected by centrifugation. Then, samples were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 12 h. After washing with PBS solution, they were dehydrated with ethanol solutions (30, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 wt%, for 10 min). Subsequently, they were dried overnight at room temperature, and then analyzed by SEM and TEM, as described below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of bacterial adhesion on the treated leather
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli, S. aureus, and A. niger bacterial suspensions were mixed with uncoated leather, HWPUA coated leather, and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 coated leather and further cultivated for 2 h at 37 °C. Then, the leather samples were treated with 2.5% glutaraldehyde and ethanol solutions (30, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 wt%) and analyzed by SEM, as described below.

The morphology of DFNS, CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, USA) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S4800, Japan). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (BET, ASAP2460, USA) were used to measure the specific surface area of DFNS and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles. Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, VECTOR-22, USA) were recorded to characterize the chemical composition of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, DFNS, DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles, HWPUA, and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes. Strain-stress curves of membranes were obtained by functional materials examination machine (Taiwan High Iron Science and Technology Stock Company). The water contact angle of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes was measured by video optical contact angle system (OCA 20, Dataphysics, Germany) using a 5 μL deionized water droplet. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles were measured by ESR spectrometer (JEOL, JES-FA200, Japan) under dark and light conditions using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine as a trapping agent and a xenon lamp (0.3 W/cm2), respectively. The light irradiation was performed for 15 min. 1H NMR spectra of HWPUA sample were measured by a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (AVANCE NEO 600 MHz, Bruker, Switzerland) in (methyl sulfoxide)-d6. The transient photocurrent response of the samples was tested on an electrochemical workstation using a general three-electrode setup (Shanghai Chenhua CHI-660D).

The CQDs, MoS2 QDs, DFNS, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanostructures were first analyzed by TEM and HRTEM. The average particle sizes of CQDs and MoS2 QDs were about 4.23 nm (Fig. 2 a and S1a, b) and 3.83 nm (Fig. 2b and S1c, d), respectively. The lattice spacings of CQDs were 0.35 and 0.25 nm, which correspond to (020) and (002) facets of graphite, respectively (Fig. 2a) [34]. As a natural polyamine, spermidine affects DNA stability, cell growth, proliferation, and causes cell death. After modification with spermidine, CQDs exhibited high charge and ROS yield. The zeta potential of CQDs was + 101.0 mV, which was because pyrolyzed polyamine spermidine molecules aggregated on the CQDs surface, resulting in increased surface cation density and high surface charge. MoS2 QDs had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of MoS2 (Fig. 2b) [38]. As shown in Fig. 2c, DFNS was made of particles with a size of about 330 nm and displayed a high number of radial channels with sizes between 6 and 41 nm. Compared with the smooth channel surface of DFNS, the channel surface of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 displayed nanoparticles and lattice fringe of CQDs and MoS2 QDs (Fig. 2d and 2e). C, N, Mo, and S elements were evenly distributed in DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles (Fig. 2i). These results confirmed that CQDs and MoS2 QDs were embedded inside the DFNS channel. Nitrogen sorption analysis was employed to characterize the porous nature of DFNS and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2. The obtained N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of DFNS and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 were of type IV (Fig. 2f) [39]. As listed in Table S1, the BET surface and the total pore volume of DFNS were 558 m2 g−1 and 1.3 cm3 g−1, respectively, and those of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 were 458 m2 g−1 and 1.1 cm3 g−1, respectively. The average pore diameter of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 was 10.8 nm, smaller than that of DFNS nanoparticles (11.4 nm) [37]. FT-IR spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups (Fig. 2h). The FT-IR spectrum of DFNS displayed the characteristic vibration bands at 1087, 804, and 465 cm−1, corresponding to the Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching, symmetric stretching, and bending vibrations, respectively. The spectrum of CQDs displayed bands at 1642 and 1559 cm−1, which were ascribed to CO stretching vibration (amide I) and NH bending vibration (amide II), respectively. These results confirmed that the CQDs were doped with N elements [40]. For the MoS2 QDs, the spectrum displayed the typical stretching vibration bands of amide I, amide II, and C-N stretching vibration (amide III) at 1676, 1590, and 1298 cm−1, respectively [35]. The spectrum of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 displayed similar vibration bands as those of DFNS, CQDs, and MoS2 QDs. DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 aqueous dispersion exhibited the excitation wavelengths of 298 and 331 nm (Fig. S2). These results provided evidence that the CQDs and MoS2 QDs were placed on the surface of the channels of DFNS.

To assess the photochemical and antibacterial properties of the CQDs and MoS2 QDs loaded on DFNS and to understand the mechanism of bacterial disruption, photocurrent measurements, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and ESR measurement were carried out. As shown in Fig. S3, the CQDs, MoS2 QDs and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles were carried out six repeated photocurrent response measurements, with and without the irradiation of simulated solar light. Upon the light illumination, DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles showed the higher value than the photocurrents of CQDs or MoS2 QDs alone, demonstrating effective separation of the photoexcited charges and limitation of their recombination. Meanwhile, EIS showed the smaller semicircle radius of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles than CQDs or MoS2 QDs (Fig. S4), indicating low impedance and fast interface charge transfer in the two QDs. This result further implied higher efficacy in separation and charge transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the ESR spectra of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 are depicted in Fig. 3 . The spin trap for singlet oxygen (1O2) was 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine. No obvious ESR signals were detected for the three materials under ambient light conditions. However, ESR signals appeared after 15 min of exposure to the xenon lamp while the ESR signal intensities were different for different samples. MoS2 QDs showed a weak ESR signal (Fig. 3a), ESR signal of CQDs was slightly higher than that of MoS2 QDs (Fig. 3b). DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 exhibited a significantly higher signal compared to CQDs and MoS2 QDs (Fig. 3c) [41–43]. This showed that the yield of ROS was significantly improved as a result of loading CQDs and MoS2 QDs on DFNS, which promoted an effective transfer of carriers, and enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 3d) [13,26,44]. It was expected that this property would have a significant impact on the antibacterial activity of the prepared nanocomposite material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of HWPUA and HWPUA/DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 membranes
Antibacterial activity of nanoparticles and coated leather
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial properties of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 were measured by the plate count method (Figs. 6. and S7). Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, Gram-negative bacteria E. coli, and fungus A. niger were selected as representative strains. The ability of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 to kill bacteria was attributed to their photocatalytic properties. The materials showed antibacterial activity similar to their photocatalytic performance. However, MoS2 QDs exhibited the lowest antibacterial activity due to its lowest photocatalytic efficiency [23]. The enhanced antibacterial performance of CQDs was attributed to the high surface charge and ROS formation [40]. DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 showed excellent antibacterial activities of 99.98, 95.35, and 99.99% for S. aureus, E. coli, and A. niger, respectively. This achievement can be explained by the synergistic effect of CQDs and MoS2 QDs.

SEM and TEM images of S. aureus cells, E. coli cells, and A. niger cells irradiated with light (0.3 W/cm2 xenon lamp) for 15 min showed that they have a smooth and intact membrane. In contrast, the treatment with DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 for 15 min caused severe destruction of the bacterial cell membranes of all three microbes, indicating the excellent bactericidal ability of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 (Fig. 7 ) [48,49]. In addition, the different damage levels of S. aureus cells and E. coli cells might be due to the different structures of the two bacteria. S. aureus is a Gram-positive bacteria, having lipoteichoic acid and peptidoglycan in the thick cell wall. E. coli is a Gram-negative bacteria with lipopolysaccharide and a lower content of peptidoglycan in the thinner cell wall. On the other hand, A. niger fungus has a different membrane structure, which contains mannoproteins, β-(1,6)-glucan, β-(1,3)-glucan, and chitin [50]. The excellent photocatalytic activity of DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 generated more ROS, which attacked the bacterial membranes, proteins, DNA, and/or RNA, finally causing the leakage of the bacterial contents and death. Therefore, a high amount of generated ROS increases the antibacterial performance. Moreover, many interactions between CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and bacterial membranes occur due to the polyamine surface of CQDs and the small size of CQDs and MoS2 QDs. The proteins, porins, and/or peptidoglycans of the bacterial cell membranes are attached to CQDs and MoS2 QDs through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Then, the cell membrane undergoes synergistic damage and inhibition [13]. Hence, the nanocomposite material composed of CQDs, MoS2 QDs, and DFNS has a significant damaging effect on bacteria.

In summary, a surface composed of HWPUA and DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles with bactericidal and anti-adhesive properties was developed. The CQDs and MoS2 QDs were embedded in DFNS pores to improve the antibacterial properties. As a carrier, DFNS not only prevented the aggregation of quantum dots but also promoted the transfer of photocarrier and generation of ROS between CQDs and MoS2. The HWPUA surface showed anti-adhesive properties and prevented biofilm formation. After DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles were loaded into HWPUA, the resulting antibacterial surface completely inactivated the bacteria. This facile strategy provides a valuable method for constructing antibacterial surfaces.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0308602), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803112).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.132170.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The challenge of emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Morens, G.K. Folkers, A.S. Fauci, The challenge of emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases, Nature 430(6996) (2004) 242-249.
G. O’Toole, H.B. Kaplan, R. Kolter, Biofilm formation as microbial development, Annu. Rev. of Microbiol. 54(1) (2000) 49-79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Control of biofilm formation: antibiotics and beyond

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Algburi, N. Comito, D. Kashtanov, L.M. Dicks, M.L. Chikindas, M.J. Pettinari, Control of biofilm formation: antibiotics and beyond, Appl. Environ. Microb. 83(3) (2016).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance of bacteria in biofilms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.S. Stewart, J.W. Costerton, Antibiotic resistance of bacteria in biofilms, Lancet 358(9276) (2001) 135-138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Device-associated infections: a macroproblem that starts with microadherence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.A. Weinstein, R.O. Darouiche, Device-associated infections: a macroproblem that starts with microadherence, Clin. Infect. Dis. 33(9) (2001) 1567-1572.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial surfaces: the quest for a new generation of biomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hasan, R.J. Crawford, E.P. Ivanova, Antibacterial surfaces: the quest for a new generation of biomaterials, Trends Biotechnol. 31(5) (2013) 295-304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Is there a threshold in the antibacterial action of superhydrophobic surfaces?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ellinas, D. Kefallinou, K. Stamatakis, E. Gogolides, A. Tserepi, Is there a threshold in the antibacterial action of superhydrophobic surfaces?, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 9(45) (2017) 39781-39789.
